audienceSections.normal: In a description of a room at home, you will use [[grammar:place + 에 + N + 이/가 있어요]]. Put [[grammar:에]] after the place and [[grammar:이/가 있어요]] after the thing that is there. 책상 옆에 의자가 있어요 means there is a chair beside the desk. Do not use this pattern to describe an action happening at a place.
audienceSections.expert: This chapter presents [[grammar:place + 에 + N + 이/가 있어요]] in a description of a room at home. Locative 에 marks the site, nominative 이/가 the theme, and informal-polite 있어요 the existential predicate. Particle selection follows the preceding coda.

audienceSections.normal: Put [[grammar:에]] after the place and [[grammar:이/가 있어요]] after the thing that is there. 책상 옆에 의자가 있어요 means there is a chair beside the desk. Do not use this pattern to describe an action happening at a place.
audienceSections.expert: Locative 에 marks the site, nominative 이/가 the theme, and informal-polite 있어요 the existential predicate. Particle selection follows the preceding coda.

audienceSections.normal: Read each Hangul block as one syllable. Particles stay attached to the word before them, but the breakdown explains what each particle does.
audienceSections.expert: The examples use standard Korean in the informal-polite 해요 style. Particle allomorphy and contractions are analyzed as grammar while lexical entries retain citation forms.

breakdown.normal:
Read each exact korean line, then check its natural English meaning. Watch for [[grammar:place + 에 + N + 이/가 있어요]] where it appears.

- 방에 책상이 있어요. — There is a desk in the room.
- 책상 위에 책이 있어요. — There is a book on the desk.
- 책상 옆에 의자가 있어요. — There is a chair beside the desk.
- 의자 위에 가방이 있어요. — There is a bag on the chair.
- 방에 창문이 있어요. — There is a window in the room.
- 창문 옆에 작은 책상이 있어요. — There is a small desk beside the window.
breakdown.expert:
